text.heading: For the Michaels, who will never be something
text.p: He was the king of pop. He came, saw, changed, and left. Did he conquer, some of us have been asking? Some have made music of his failings. Some have created stories of his frailties. Many others have feasted on his victories. All have their views; some sound, some warped and many others bizarrely illogical. But then, who made us judges over him? Who?

Who made us judges over Michael Jackson? Who? Yes, he had problems with being an adult. He had challenges forgetting the scars of childhood; he could not handle well his ‘blackness,’ some have said. It was tough getting to the top, tougher staying there, we all know. In the midst of the Babel of voices surrounding Michael Jackson though, it cannot be lost on any that he created a Jackson, out of an ordinary Michael. He made a person out of his being. He was not lost in the crowd, he stood out. That is the tragedy of the other Michaels.

Across the globe, continents far and near, countries hidden and prominent, towns and cities, here and there, there are several Michaels trying to model their lives around others. They seek, without knowing, to create a new personality out of their perceived blighted self. Rightly, they are not comfortable with their present situation like Jackson. They want to change it. They however do not know how.

You will see them in the music sector with dreadlocks, the type made popular by Bob Marley. They love good music, but are not ready to make them. At parties, they mime others and are too busy to create their own. They take weed, ganja and other harmful substances as if these breed greatness. These other Michaels love good things but are far too concerned about the bad ways around good deeds. They end up either in various psychiatric homes or on the streets as ‘children of government’ as the Yoruba refer to them.

One thing that stands these Michaels out in Nigeria is their love for all that is alien and a commensurate disdain for all that is ours. They have accents that are unfamiliar to all; mannerisms that no one can link to any civilisation; a dress sense that no one can identify with and slang that are either too weird or too loose. They are pursuers of the shallow things around stardom. In fact they crave the stars but are not ready to lift themselves off ground zero.

In sports, they cheat at every turn. They are 16 on their 30th birthdays; under-20s when they are over 30. They help themselves to drugs instead of work. They lobby to be selected; and then pay to keep in the limelight. Whenever talent is to be celebrated, they show their latency for quackery. For every failure, they craft some tricky excuses. They do not care to prepare to excel, after all; you could blame others for failure.

In the academia, they work their ways to make qualifying grades; do more to move on and end up selling everything from marks to handouts, all to keep the pretence to intellectualism. They ape every move of those in authority; ask no questions, so deserve no answers. They are like robots in the hands of power, pliable and manoeuvrable all the time. They have no elastic limits; they take abuses and rape and give as much to their hapless students. They struggle for nothing and seek to benefit from everything. Conscience, to them, is alien, so there is no truth to be healed around them.

In politics, they are examples of all that is vain. Some bleach their skins only to woo their next consorts; steal as if today is the last. They think only about aspiring to power. They kill to get into office, murder to stay there. They do everything that is wrong, pretending they want to right the past. They claim they want to take us forward while they are driving us backward.

They dream dreams of the past and present them as hope for the future. The Michaels in the political firmament are not concerned about today, they care little about tomorrow; they are so steeped in inglorious fantasies about themselves. They are verbose when they should be taciturn and reticent when they ought to speak out. They are meticulous concerning frivolity and flippant on matters of grave concern. They speak when they ought to be listening and keep sealed lips when they ought to speak out.

They are there in journalism; as quacks selling their by-lines for a kobo. In medical practice they are there, in law they are many. In all their areas of endeavour, the Michaels never dared to be different. When they do attempt brilliance, it is in breaking all records of indecency, throwing politeness and decorum to the dogs. They are therefore always lost in the crowd, or consigned to infamy. Unlike Michael Jackson, the other Michaels are comfortable with just being there.

And so as their voices are lost in condemning the excellence that Michael Jackson was, we write about the men and women who have refused to move beyond their limitations; who have refused to live above their weaknesses and have become prisoners of their circumstances. They may live to be a million years, but they will leave this part unsung, uncelebrated; alone, and go either to heaven or to hell, unheralded.
